# Architecture Decision Record Standard

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

## The Standard

Every significant architecture decision SHOULD be recorded in a structured,
discoverable format that captures context, constraints, options considered,
the decision made, and expected consequences. A decision is "significant"
when reversing it later would be expensive, when it constrains future
choices, or when it crosses team boundaries.

The record exists to serve future readers — people who were not in the room,
including the decision-makers themselves six months later. The record must
therefore be self-contained: a reader should not need tribal knowledge to
understand why the decision was made.

## What Good Looks Like

- Every significant architecture decision has a written record stored in
  a known, consistent location within the repository or knowledge base.
- Each record follows a structured format: title, date, status (proposed,
  accepted, deprecated, superseded), context, options evaluated, decision,
  rationale, and consequences.
- Records are discoverable by anyone with access to the codebase — no
  special permissions, no hidden wikis, no oral tradition required.
- Decisions reference the constraints and forces that shaped them, so
  future readers can judge whether the forces have changed.
- Superseded decisions link to their replacements, preserving the chain
  of reasoning rather than silently overwriting history.
- Decisions are reviewed at technology lifecycle milestones — when a
  technology moves between lifecycle stages, the decisions that placed
  it there are revisited.

## Gap Analysis Guide

- **No records exist.** The team relies on institutional memory. This is
  the most common and most dangerous gap — it guarantees knowledge decay.
- **Records exist but lack context.** The "what" is captured but not the
  "why." Future readers cannot evaluate whether the decision still holds.
- **Records are scattered.** Some live in wikis, some in Slack threads,
  some in pull request descriptions. No single index exists.
- **Records are write-once.** Decisions are recorded but never revisited.
  Deprecated decisions are not marked. Superseded decisions have no links.
- **Records lack options analysis.** Only the chosen path is documented.
  Without rejected alternatives, the decision looks arbitrary.

## 1970/2070 Note

Whether decisions are carved in stone tablets or stored in neural
interfaces, the need to record why a choice was made persists. The medium
changes; the discipline of capturing context, alternatives, and rationale
does not. Engineering organizations in any era benefit from externalizing
decision logic so it survives personnel turnover.

## Baseline+Overlay Note

This eng-domain standard extends JOS's universal governance. It operates
in addition to, not instead of, meta-layer standards.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-P19 — the principle this standard implements
- JOS-C07 (Knowledge Decay) — the concept that makes decision records essential

### Framework Connection

Framework §15 (Decision as a HAPPENS-axis work type) positions architecture decisions within the broader work type taxonomy, giving ADRs a formal place in the governance structure alongside other work types like incidents and reviews.
